title: Pragmatism
description: Pragmatism — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Pragmatism" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Absolutism
memes.description: The epistemological doctrine that there exists an absolute, objective standard of truth independent of human perspectives and practices — contrasted with relativism and criticized by pragmatism as an unusable ideal.

memes.label: Agnosticism
memes.description: The suspension of belief regarding God's existence due to insufficient evidence, which James critiques as practically equivalent to atheism in its life-consequences.

memes.label: Alfred North Whitehead
memes.description: British mathematician and philosopher who later developed process philosophy; co-authored Principia Mathematica with Bertrand Russell and later influenced American pragmatism.

memes.label: Gifford Lectures
memes.description: Prestigious lecture series on natural theology established in 1887 at the Scottish universities, where William James delivered the lectures that became Pragmatism (1907).

memes.label: Apriorism
memes.description: The epistemological doctrine that certain fundamental principles of knowledge are innate _a priori_ truths of reason, independent of and prior to experience — the chief rival to both empiricism and the pragmatist doctrine of postulation.

memes.label: Arthur Schopenhauer
memes.description: German philosopher whose metaphysics of the will and philosophical pessimism influenced Nietzsche and, indirectly, the pragmatist critique of rationalism.

memes.label: Auguste Comte
memes.description: French philosopher and founder of positivism and sociology; his doctrine that knowledge progresses through theological, metaphysical, and positive stages influenced the pragmatist theory of inquiry.

memes.label: Axioms as Postulates
memes.description: F.C.S. Schiller's 1902 essay (later expanded) arguing that logical axioms are not self-evident truths but postulates adopted for their practical utility — a foundational text of voluntarist epistemology

memes.label: Belief
memes.description: In James's pragmatism, belief is a volitional attitude toward an idea — an act of will that is tested by its practical consequences, not a passive reflection of self-evident truth.

memes.label: George Berkeley
memes.description: Irish philosopher whose idealist empiricism — that to be is to be perceived — represents a crucial waystation between Lockean empiricism and Hegelian absolutism.

memes.label: British Associationism
memes.description: The psychological school, rooted in Hume and developed by James Mill and Alexander Bain, that explained all mental life through the laws of association — the framework William James's "New Psychology" aimed to replace.

memes.label: British Empiricism
memes.description: The tradition of Locke, Berkeley, and Hume that traced all knowledge to sensory experience — the philosophical ancestor that pragmatism both inherited and transformed.

memes.label: Charles Darwin
memes.description: British naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection was identified by William James as one of the foundational intellectual currents feeding into Pragmatism — "another fountain-head of Pragmatic philosophy."

memes.label: Charles Sanders Peirce
memes.description: American philosopher, logician, and scientist who originated the pragmatic maxim and founded the philosophical tradition of pragmatism.

memes.label: Chauncey Wright
memes.description: American philosopher and mathematician; a key member of the Metaphysical Club whose empiricist and Darwinian views deeply influenced the early pragmatists.

memes.label: Chicago School of Pragmatism
memes.description: "The group of American philosophers centered at the University of Chicago, led by John Dewey, whose logical theories treated thought as an instrument of evolutionary readjustment. James identifies the Chicago School as the primary philosophical exponent of Darwinism's influence on pragmatism (PN 001-108)."

memes.label: Common Sense
memes.description: In pragmatism, common sense is the inherited stock of practical beliefs about the world — not metaphysical truth but evolutionary instruments for navigating experience.

memes.label: Common Sense Philosophy
memes.description: The Scottish philosophical tradition, inaugurated by Thomas Reid, that defended the reliability of common-sense beliefs against Humean skepticism — a recurring ally in James's pragmatic argument against radical empiricist doubt.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Index of all concept entries in the Pragmatism wiki — philosophical terms and ideas discussed across William James's lectures.

memes.label: Consciousness
memes.description: The continuous, flowing quality of mental life that William James described as the "stream of consciousness," foundational to his psychology and pragmatic philosophy.

memes.label: Correspondence Theory of Truth
memes.description: The traditional philosophical theory that truth consists in the correspondence of a thought or assertion to reality, critically examined by William James in Pragmatism.

memes.label: Critique of Pure Reason
memes.description: Immanuel Kant's 1781/1787 masterwork that redefined the limits and possibilities of human knowledge, and the primary target of pragmatism's revolt against transcendental philosophy.

memes.label: Darwinian Revolution
memes.description: The transformation of biology and philosophy following Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859), which deeply shaped pragmatism's evolutionary approach to knowledge, truth, and mind.

memes.label: Darwinism
memes.description: The evolutionary theory of Charles Darwin, identified by James as a principal intellectual source of pragmatism, which replaced static, fixed conceptions of species and truth with dynamic, evolving, adaptive ones.

memes.label: David Hume
memes.description: David Hume (1711–1776) was the Scottish empiricist philosopher whose atomistic psychology and sceptical conclusions defined the philosophical problem that William James's pragmatism sets out to overcome.

memes.label: René Descartes
memes.description: French philosopher and mathematician whose rationalism established the intellectualist tradition that James's pragmatism seeks to overcome.

memes.label: Determinism
memes.description: The doctrine that all events are necessitated by prior causes, which William James argued against on pragmatic grounds as incompatible with moral effort and meliorism.

memes.label: Dogmatism
memes.description: The naive and instinctive conviction that one's own beliefs are self-evidently or indisputably true, which pragmatism identifies as the root of rationalist philosophy and the source of its characteristic dilemmas.

memes.label: Empiricism
memes.description: Empiricism is the epistemological doctrine that all knowledge derives from sensory experience — the position that James's pragmatism seeks to transcend as a "third alternative" to the banal dilemma of empiricism versus apriorism.

memes.label: Ernst Mach
memes.description: Austrian physicist and philosopher whose radical empiricism and critique of absolute space influenced James's pragmatic conception of scientific truth.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Index of all event entries in the Pragmatism wiki — historical events and academic lectures referenced in William James's lectures.

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: The continuous flux of conscious life from which knowledge selects and organizes percepts — the foundational datum that pragmatism radically reinterpreted against both classical empiricism and apriorism.

memes.label: F.C.S. Schiller
memes.description: British pragmatist philosopher who coined the term "Humanism" and developed a voluntarist theory of knowledge.

memes.label: F.H. Bradley
memes.description: British idealist philosopher whose Appearance and Reality was the definitive statement of absolute idealism in the English-speaking world.

memes.label: Formal Logic
memes.description: F.C.S. Schiller's 1912 critique of traditional formal logic from a pragmatic standpoint, arguing that logic must account for meaning, purpose, and the concrete processes of human thought
aliases: Formal Logic: A Scientific and Social Problem

memes.label: Free Will
memes.description: The doctrine that human choices are not fully determined by prior causes, central to James's voluntarism and the argument of The Will to Believe.

memes.label: George Santayana
memes.description: Spanish-American philosopher and poet whose naturalism and critique of the "genteel tradition" paralleled and contrasted with James's pragmatism.

memes.label: German Idealism
memes.description: The philosophical movement originating with Kant and culminating in Hegel that dominated German philosophy from the 1780s to the 1840s — the primary target of pragmatism's anti-intellectualist revolt.

memes.label: Gifford Lectures
memes.description: Prestigious lecture series on natural theology established in 1887 at the Scottish universities, where William James delivered the lectures that became Pragmatism (1907).

memes.label: Giovanni Papini
memes.description: Italian pragmatist philosopher and writer who led the Italian pragmatist movement and coined the metaphor of pragmatism as a "corridor" between philosophical rooms.

memes.label: G.W.F. Hegel
memes.description: German idealist philosopher whose absolute idealism was the dominant philosophical system that James's pragmatism directly challenged.

memes.label: Henri Bergson
memes.description: French philosopher (1859–1941), leading figure in the philosophy of time, consciousness, and creative evolution; a close contemporary and ally of William James.

memes.label: Herbert Spencer
memes.description: British philosopher and sociologist whose evolutionary philosophy dominated late 19th-century thought, providing both inspiration and a foil for William James's pragmatism.

memes.label: Humanism
memes.description: The pragmatist doctrine, developed by F.C.S. Schiller and endorsed by James, that all knowledge and truth are human products shaped by human purposes, interests, and valuations — reality is always reality as experienced and selected by human minds.

memes.label: Hypothesis
memes.description: A provisional belief or postulate adopted for the purpose of testing its consequences, central to William James's pragmatic account of scientific and philosophical method.

memes.label: Idealism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine that reality is fundamentally mental or spiritual, which William James treats as the tender-minded extreme that pragmatism mediates against.

memes.label: Immanuel Kant
memes.description: German Enlightenment philosopher whose transcendental idealism and Critique of Pure Reason attempted to reconcile rationalism and empiricism — a pivotal figure whose a priori categories and faculty psychology are critically examined and rejected by James's pragmatism

memes.label: Intellectualism
memes.description: The philosophical tendency to privilege abstract reasoning and formal systems over concrete experience, practical consequences, and lived reality — a central target of pragmatist critique.

memes.label: Intuition
memes.description: The claimed ability to apprehend truth directly without reasoning or sensory evidence, subjected to pragmatic critique in James's lectures.

memes.label: James Mill
memes.description: Scottish philosopher, historian, and political theorist; father of John Stuart Mill and a key figure in British empiricism and associationist psychology.

memes.label: John Dewey
memes.description: "American philosopher and leading figure of the Chicago School of Pragmatism, whose logical theories James identifies as deeply inspired by Darwinian evolution (PN 001-108)."

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: English philosopher whose empiricism provided the foundation for the tradition that James's pragmatism both inherits and transforms.

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: British philosopher whose empiricism and liberalism deeply influenced James, who dedicated Pragmatism to Mill's memory.

memes.label: Josiah Royce
memes.description: American absolute idealist philosopher, Harvard colleague and friendly philosophical adversary of William James.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: In Jamesian pragmatism, knowledge is neither passive reception of external facts nor an a priori mental necessity but a purposive, experimental contribution of the mind to the organization of experience — tested by its vital consequences.

memes.label: "I. The Present Dilemma in Philosophy"
memes.description: "Lecture I: The Present Dilemma in Philosophy — the tough-minded vs tender-minded"

memes.label: "II. What Pragmatism Means"
memes.description: "Lecture II: What Pragmatism Means — the pragmatic method and the squirrel dispute"

memes.label: "III. Some Metaphysical Problems Pragmatically Considered"
memes.description: "Lecture III: Some Metaphysical Problems Pragmatically Considered — substance, materialism, design"

memes.label: "IV. The One and the Many"
memes.description: "Lecture IV: The One and the Many — monism vs pluralism, rationalism vs empiricism"

memes.label: "V. Pragmatism and Common Sense"
memes.description: "Lecture V: Pragmatism and Common Sense — common sense categories, their origins and uses"

memes.label: "VI. Pragmatism's Conception of Truth"
memes.description: "Lecture VI: Pragmatism's Conception of Truth — truth as verification, the credit system of truths"

memes.label: "VII. Pragmatism and Humanism"
memes.description: "Lecture VII: Pragmatism and Humanism — humanism, rationalism, the making of reality"

memes.label: "VIII. Pragmatism and Religion"
memes.description: "Lecture VIII: Pragmatism and Religion — the religious hypothesis, meliorism, the right to believe"

relations.label: The Will to Believe, and Other Essays in Popular Philosophy

memes.label: G.W. Leibniz
memes.description: German rationalist philosopher and mathematician whose pluralistic metaphysics of monads and doctrine of the best possible world anticipated and contrasted with themes in James's pragmatism.

memes.label: Logic
memes.description: The formal study of valid reasoning and inference, and its critique by pragmatist philosophers who argued that logic must be grounded in concrete meaning and practical consequences.

memes.label: Materialism
memes.description: The metaphysical doctrine that only matter exists and all phenomena can be explained in terms of material processes — the tough-minded extreme that James treats as the empiricist counterpart to absolute idealism.

memes.label: Meliorism
memes.description: "The philosophical view that reality is neither fully good nor fully bad but can be improved through human effort — a position between optimism and pessimism central to James's pragmatic Humanism."

memes.label: Metaphysical Club
memes.description: "Informal discussion group at Cambridge, Massachusetts, in the early 1870s, widely recognized as the birthplace of pragmatism."

memes.label: Monism
memes.description: The metaphysical view that all of reality is ultimately one substance or system, which William James critiques as the tender-minded extreme that pragmatism must temper with pluralistic empiricism.

memes.label: Mysticism
memes.description: Direct experiential knowledge of divine or ultimate reality, which James treats as empirical data for the pragmatic assessment of religion in The Varieties of Religious Experience.

memes.label: Naturalism
memes.description: The philosophical view that nature is all there is and all phenomena can be explained by natural causes, which James both inherits and transforms through his pragmatic method.

memes.label: Nominalism
memes.description: The view that universals are mere names without independent reality, which James aligns pragmatism with against conceptualism and Platonic realism.

memes.label: Optimism
memes.description: The tender-minded belief that this world is the best possible, which James treats as one pole of the philosophical temperament spectrum alongside pessimism.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Index of philosophical schools, movements, and organizations discussed in the Pragmatism lectures.

memes.label: Oxford Pragmatism
memes.description: The early 20th-century philosophical movement centered at Oxford University, led by F.C.S. Schiller and Henry Sturt, which developed a distinctively British form of pragmatism under the names "Humanism" and "Personal Idealism."

memes.label: Pantheism
memes.description: The identification of God with the universe as a whole — the religious correlate of absolute idealism that James critiques as pragmatically indistinguishable from atheism.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Index of all person entries — philosophers and thinkers referenced in James's Pragmatism lectures.

memes.label: Personal Idealism
memes.description: A 1902 collection of essays edited by Henry Sturt, containing F.C.S. Schiller's "Axioms as Postulates" and other works representing a shift from Absolute Idealism toward personalist and pragmatist philosophy in Britain

memes.label: Pessimism
memes.description: The tough-minded belief that the world is fundamentally bad or indifferent to human welfare, representing the empiricist pole in James's philosophical temperament typology.

memes.label: Philosophy
memes.description: The role and nature of philosophy as understood through William James's Pragmatism, emphasizing its practical consequences and evolutionary character.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher whose theory of Forms and the problem of defining knowledge in the Theaetetus represent the starting point of Western epistemology — a tradition pragmatism seeks to overcome

memes.label: Pluralism
memes.description: The metaphysical view that reality consists of many independent entities rather than one unified whole, representing William James's preferred alternative to absolute monism.

memes.label: Positivism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine that only scientifically verifiable claims are meaningful, which James treats as an influential but incomplete account of human knowledge.

memes.label: Postulation
memes.description: A methodological doctrine holding that first principles are freely chosen postulates which are tested and verified by their success in organizing experience, as opposed to being either empirical generalizations or a priori necessities.

memes.label: Pragmatic Method
memes.description: The pragmatist procedure for settling philosophical disputes by tracing the practical consequences of competing ideas — if no practical difference can be found, the dispute is meaningless.

memes.label: Pragmatism
memes.description: A philosophical tradition that evaluates theories and beliefs in terms of their practical consequences and applications.

memes.label: "The Principles of Psychology"
memes.description: William James's monumental 1890 treatise that revolutionized psychology by replacing Hume's atomistic sensationalism with the stream of consciousness and introducing biological and voluntaristic principles that paved the way for pragmatism.

memes.label: Psychology
memes.description: The role of psychology in James's Pragmatism, from Hume's empirical psychology to the "new psychology" that transformed philosophical method and the theory of knowledge.

memes.label: Publication of Pragmatism (1907)
memes.description: The 1907 publication of William James's Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking, originally delivered as the Lowell Lectures in Boston, which launched pragmatism as a public philosophical movement.

memes.label: Radical Empiricism
memes.description: William James's later philosophical doctrine that experience includes not only sensory data but also the relations between experiences, requiring a "radical criticism" of traditional empiricism's atomistic assumptions.

memes.label: Rationalism
memes.description: The philosophical tradition, from Descartes through Leibniz to Kant and Hegel, that holds reason to be the primary source of knowledge — in James's Pragmatism, the chief rival that pragmatism aims to mediate between and ultimately overcome.

memes.label: Rationality
memes.description: In Jamesian pragmatism, rationality is not a static faculty of pure reason but a purposive, selective activity by which the mind postulates order upon experience and tests it through practical consequences.

memes.label: Reality
memes.description: In pragmatism, reality is not a static given but a plastic, human-shaped selection from experience, always purposive and open to improvement through action.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: In James's pragmatism, "Reason" (capitalized as the intellectualist ideal of a pure, dehumanized faculty) is critiqued as an untenable abstraction — real reasoning is always purposive, selective, and psychologically situated.

memes.label: Relativism
memes.description: The epistemological view that truth-claims can only be compared and evaluated against one another without appeal to an external absolute standard — contrasted with absolutism and, in James's analysis, vindicated by the pragmatist method.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: William James's Pragmatic treatment of religion as a domain of human experience subject to the same pragmatic method of verification as science, grounded in postulates and testable by practical consequences.

memes.label: Religious Experience
memes.description: The personal, experiential dimension of religious belief, central to William James's pragmatic philosophy and his psychological study of religion.

memes.label: Scepticism
memes.description: The philosophical position that knowledge is uncertain or impossible, examined by William James as a consequence of absolutist and intellectualist theories of truth.

memes.label: Science
memes.description: In James's pragmatism, science is not a body of immutable truths but a policy of inquiry — a set of working formulae for mastering the flux of events, whose laws are man-made instruments tested by predictive success rather than transcripts of an eternal order.

memes.label: Sensation
memes.description: The atomistic unit of sensory experience in classical empiricism, critiqued by James's radical empiricism as an artificial abstraction from the continuous stream of consciousness.

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: Dutch rationalist philosopher whose pantheistic monism provided important precedents for both absolute idealism and the radical empiricism of William James.

memes.label: Stream of Consciousness
memes.description: William James's metaphor for the continuous, flowing nature of conscious experience, in which each moment carries its past along and thought moves as a unified stream rather than a chain of discrete ideas.

memes.label: Studies in Humanism
memes.description: F.C.S. Schiller's 1907 monograph developing Humanism as a comprehensive philosophical position that treats all knowledge and truth as human products shaped by purpose, valuation, and selective interest.

memes.label: The Absolute
memes.description: The Absolute is the idealist postulate of a perfect, all-encompassing divine mind whose experience constitutes ultimate reality and truth — a central target of James's pragmatic critique.

memes.label: The Varieties of Religious Experience
memes.description: William James's 1902 Gifford Lectures, a landmark psychological study of religious experience that applied pragmatist methods to questions of faith, mysticism, and saintliness.

memes.label: "The Will to Believe"
memes.description: "William James's 1896 essay defending the legitimacy of religious faith in the absence of conclusive evidence, published as the title essay in his 1897 collection. A foundational text for pragmatist epistemology of religion."
aliases: and Other Essays in Popular Philosophy

memes.label: Theism
memes.description: Belief in a personal God who is both transcendent and active in the world, treated by William James as a "live hypothesis" whose truth is pragmatically verifiable through its consequences in human experience.

memes.label: Thomas Reid
memes.description: Scottish philosopher and founder of the Scottish School of Common Sense; a major critic of Hume's skepticism and an influence on American pragmatism through his appeal to common sense.

memes.label: Transcendentalism
memes.description: The philosophical movement, originating in Kant's critical philosophy and branching into New England Transcendentalism, that sought to identify the a priori conditions of experience — a tradition James regarded as an ingenious but ultimately futile attempt to repair the defects of empiricism.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: In pragmatism, truth is not a static property of propositions but something that happens to an idea — it becomes true through events and practical verification.

memes.label: Verification
memes.description: The pragmatic process of testing beliefs through experience and consequences, central to William James's theory of truth and knowledge in Pragmatism.

memes.label: William James
memes.description: American philosopher and psychologist (1842–1910), founder of pragmatism and radical empiricism, author of Pragmatism (1907) and The Principles of Psychology (1890).

memes.label: W.K. Clifford
memes.description: British mathematician and philosopher whose evidentialist principle — that it is wrong to believe anything on insufficient evidence — was the direct target of James's The Will to Believe.

memes.label: Work Index
memes.description: Index of books and publications referenced in James's Pragmatism lectures.
